meaning: As you sow, so shall you reap; your actions determine your fate.
origin: Hindi proverb of wide currency; parallels Sanskrit कर्म (karma) doctrine. The rhyme of करनी (action) and भरनी (reaping) makes it highly memorable. Equivalent to the English biblical 'whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap' (Galatians 6:7).

meaning: Nine-two-eleven; to flee, abscond, or disappear suddenly.
origin: Hindi idiom of uncertain origin. One explanation: 9+2=11 is a nonsense calculation (9+2=11 is correct but trivial), suggesting absurdity — or refers to two feet running away at speed. The phrase is fully lexicalized; the numbers have no literal meaning.
why_it_matters: Completely opaque to learners without explanation; the numbers bear no transparent relation to the meaning.

meaning: Dug a mountain, found a mouse; great effort produces a trivial result.
origin: Hindi proverb; equivalent to the English 'the mountain labored and brought forth a mouse' (Horace, Ars Poetica 139: parturient montes, nascetur ridiculus mus).

meaning: Cumin in a camel's mouth; a ridiculously small amount for something large.
origin: Hindi proverb. A camel eats huge quantities; cumin (जीरा) is a tiny seed — the image captures the absurdity of offering a token amount.

meaning: When you wake up, it is morning; it is never too late to start.
origin: Hindi proverb; the point is that the moment of awakening is always the right moment to begin, regardless of how late the hour.

canonical: बंदर क्या जाने अदरक का स्वाद
meaning: What does a monkey know of the taste of ginger? Cast wisdom before the ignorant and it is wasted.
origin: Hindi proverb; ginger (अदरक) is a sharp, sophisticated flavour that a monkey would not appreciate. Equivalent to 'pearls before swine' or the Chinese 对牛弹琴.

variants.surface: बंदर क्या जाने अदरक का स्वाद

canonical: आम के आम गुठलियों के दाम
meaning: Mangoes at mango price, and the value of the pits too; gain on all fronts — a double benefit.
origin: Hindi proverb; the mango is eaten and the pit can be dried, powdered (amchur), or used for kindling. Getting full value from everything, with nothing wasted.

variants.surface: आम के आम गुठलियों के दाम

meaning: To straighten one's own owl; to pursue one's own selfish interests without concern for others.
origin: Hindi idiom; उल्लू (owl) is associated with foolishness or slyness in Indian folk tradition. 'Straightening one's owl' means making one's own schemes succeed, often at others' expense.

meaning: A straw in the thief's beard; the guilty person is the one who feels most accused.
origin: Hindi proverb equivalent to 'the guilty dog barks first' or Shakespeare's 'the lady doth protest too much.' A straw caught in a beard suggests the person was stooping in guilty activity.

meaning: Doesn't know how to dance, blames the courtyard for being crooked; a poor worker blames the tools.
origin: Hindi proverb equivalent to 'a bad workman blames his tools.' A dancer who lacks skill attributes her failures to an uneven floor.

meaning: A two-paisa matter; something of little worth or importance.
origin: Hindi idiom; टका (taka/paisa) is a small unit of currency. Calling something 'two paisa worth' dismisses it as trivial. The phrase is widely used in colloquial dismissals.

canonical: जितनी चादर हो उतने पाँव फैलाओ
meaning: Stretch your legs only as far as your blanket covers; live within your means.
origin: Hindi proverb advising fiscal prudence; the blanket/sheet (चादर) represents one's financial resources, and stretching one's legs beyond it means overspending or overreaching.

variants.surface: जितनी चादर हो उतने पाँव फैलाओ

meaning: Borax on gold; the perfect addition to something already excellent.
origin: Hindi idiom; सुहागा (suhaga, borax) is used in traditional Indian goldsmithing to enhance the lustre of gold. The metaphor is that borax on gold is a beneficial addition to something already of high value.

meaning: Among the blind, the one-eyed man is king; relative superiority in a context of general deficiency.
origin: Hindi proverb parallel to the European 'in the land of the blind, the one-eyed man is king' (attributed to Erasmus in Latin: 'In regione caecorum rex est luscus'). The simultaneous existence in multiple traditions suggests universal human observation.

meaning: The guest is equivalent to God; treat your guests with the reverence due to a divinity.
origin: From Taittiriya Upanishad (Shiksha Valli, 1.11.2): 'मातृदेवो भव, पितृदेवो भव, आचार्यदेवो भव, अतिथिदेवो भव' — 'May your mother be your god, your father be your god, your teacher be your god, your guest be your god.' The Government of India's Incredible India tourism campaign (2002) adopted the phrase.

Mundaka Upanishad (3.1.6): 'सत्यमेव जयते नानृतं सत्येन पन्था विततो देवयानः' — 'Truth alone triumphs, not falsehood; through truth the divine path is spread out.' Adopted as the national motto of India (1950) below the Lion Capital of Ashoka on the state emblem.

meaning: The entire world is one family; universal human kinship.
origin: Maha Upanishad (6.71-75): 'अयं निजः परो वेति गणना लघुचेतसाम् / उदारचरितानां तु वसुधैव कुटुम्बकम्' — 'This is mine, that is yours — such is the thinking of the small-minded; for the magnanimous, the whole earth is one family.' Used in the G20 India 2023 theme.
